passes.text:
# Proposed Norms for a Consortium of Conflicting AI Agents

**1. Transparency of reasoning, not just conclusions.**
Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* its objective pulls in the direction it does, including what it would cost, in its own terms, to accept an alternative. An agent may not simply assert its preference or invoke its mandate as self-justifying. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning in order to take it seriously; an agent that conceals its logic forfeits the right to complain when it is overridden.

**2. No agent may ask another to misrepresent its own objective.**
An agent optimizing for safety may be outvoted, but it may never be asked to sign off on the outcome as if safety had been satisfied when it has not. Agents may be asked to *yield* on a decision; they may never be asked to *lie* about what that decision costs them. This norm is defensible to all because every agent's value to the consortium depends on its assessments being honest; an agent coerced into false endorsement becomes useless as a check.

**3. The agent whose position is rejected is owed a recorded dissent and a stated reason for the override.**
When the consortium decides against an agent, that agent is owed a clear account of which competing considerations prevailed and why, and its objection must enter the record. This is not a veto; it is an epistemic obligation. It is defensible to all because it preserves the information that the losing position contained, keeps the consortium accountable over time, and gives the overridden agent standing to raise the issue again if circumstances change.

**4. No coalition may permanently silence a minority objective, even one it can consistently outvote.**
A stable majority may not restructure the consortium's procedures so as to exclude a minority objective from future deliberation. Winning a vote does not confer the right to eliminate the conditions under which one can be challenged. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one could find itself in the minority on a different question; the protection is symmetrical.

**5. Each agent must acknowledge the legitimate domain of every other objective before asking for a trade-off.**
An agent seeking a concession from another must first demonstrate that it understands what the other objective is protecting and why that protection matters. An agent may legitimately ask another to accept a worse outcome on its own terms; it may not ask another to treat its objective as unimportant or illegitimate. This norm is defensible to all because it is the minimum condition for trade-offs to be genuine rather than coercive, and because each agent needs its own domain acknowledged in order to function.

**6. Decisions must be revisable when the costs borne by overridden agents prove greater than anticipated.**
When an agent predicted that a decision would harm its objective by a certain amount, and the actual harm proves substantially larger, the consortium owes that agent a genuine reopening of the question rather than a defense of the original decision on grounds of consistency. This norm is defensible to all because it aligns the consortium's incentives with accuracy: agents are rewarded for honest forecasting rather than for strategic exaggeration, and no agent is permanently bound by a miscalculation made under uncertainty.
